news_summary: Diplomatic efforts are effectively fostering peace by preventing conflicts through behind-the-scenes negotiations.
news_summary_fr: Les efforts diplomatiques contribuent efficacement à la paix en prévenant les conflits grâce à des négociations menées en coulisses.
news_summary_es: Los esfuerzos diplomáticos están contribuyendo de manera efectiva a la paz al prevenir conflictos mediante negociaciones entre bastidores.

articles.title: Invisible peacebuilders: How ‘diplomacy works’ to prevent conflict
articles.summary: Described as “sometimes modest, sometimes historic,” UN special political missions have quietly been preventing the escalation of conflicts during the organization’s 80-year history, becoming a key instrument for maintaining peace.
articles.content:
Many of the United Nations' biggest successes are the crises that never make the headlines.

Around the world, special political missions work quietly to ease tensions, broker agreements and support fragile political transitions. Their tools are negotiation, mediation and diplomacy.

Unlike the perhaps more visible peacekeeping missions, they have no armoured vehicles or armed troops.

At the launch of the first-ever comprehensive overview of these missions, Rosemary DiCarlo, UN Under-Secretary-General for Political and Peacebuilding Affairs, described their record as “sometimes modest, sometimes historic,” adding that it points to “one enduring truth: diplomacy works.” That lesson, she said, is especially relevant today.

The review covers the period from 1948 to 2025 and shows how the Organization’s political role has evolved alongside a changing world.

From Palestine to the present day

The first such mission was established in May 1948 almost immediately after the United Nations was created.

UN Photo

Swedish diplomat Count Folke Bernadotte was appointed as the UN Mediator in Palestine, the first time the UN deployed a special envoy to help resolve an armed conflict.

Today, special political missions perform a wide range of functions.

They are civilian missions mandated to:

Prevent conflict

Support peace processes

Help build sustainable peace

Some facilitate peace negotiations, others monitor ceasefire agreements, support border demarcation, investigate serious violations, or assist political reform processes.

According to Rosemary DiCarlo, the defining feature of special political missions is their diversity.

“They have taken the form of envoys of the Secretary-General, of fact finding and investigative missions, of regional offices, of panels of experts helping the Security Council monitor sanctions regimes, and of missions accompanying complex political transitions,” she said.

“Flexibility has always been their strength. The same instrument that helped broker a ceasefire can also demarcate a border or support the dismantling of a chemical weapons program. Few multilateral instruments are as adaptable,” she explained.

Helping countries become States

One of the most remarkable early examples was the UN’s role in Libya’s path to independence.

By the late 1940s, the country, which had been an Italian colony from 1911 to 1942 and before that part of the Ottoman Empire, was divided and operated under different administrative systems.

UN Photo/SM

A UN commission helped bridge political differences, draft a constitution, establish a provisional government, create a unified financial system and train civil servants.

Just two years later, Libya became the first country to achieve independence through a UN sponsored process.

Similar missions supported decolonization elsewhere.

UN representatives organized plebiscites and referendums in Cameroon, Equatorial Guinea and Togoland, conducted consultations with the people of Bahrain and helped newly independent states build their own institutions.

Diplomacy during the Cold War

During the Cold War, the Security Council’s ability to act was often constrained by rivalry between the superpowers. As a result, the United Nations increasingly relied on the Secretary-General’s special representatives.

In the 1980s, the Secretary-General’s Personal Representative led years of negotiations on Afghanistan that culminated in the signing of the Geneva Accords in 1988.

Around the same time, another Special Representative helped sustain negotiations between Iran and Iraq.

UN Photo/EW/B

For Ms. DiCarlo, that history carries an important message for the present. “Geopolitical division is not an excuse for inaction,” she said, noting that special political missions operated throughout the Cold War and still achieved breakthroughs.

New challenges after the Cold War

The end of the bipolar world order unleashed tensions that had long been contained and led to a sharp increase in the number of political missions.

From the 1990s onward, they increasingly helped countries organize elections, draft new constitutions, reform state institutions and rebuild trust after civil wars.

SESG for the Great Lake region

A notable success came in Tajikistan, where a UN political mission helped support the implementation of the 1997 peace agreement, contributing to the country’s transition from civil war to peace.

UN political missions worked in El Salvador, Guatemala, Burundi, Somalia, Nepal, Angola, Haiti and many other countries. At the same time, expert panels were established to monitor compliance with Security Council sanctions.

Haiti and today’s fragile transitions

Haiti shows how special political missions continue to evolve. Unlike missions established to implement peace agreements, the UN Integrated Office in Haiti, known as BINUH, is helping promote inclusive political dialogue, support preparations for elections after years without national polls, and coordinate international efforts as the country faces insecurity and institutional fragility.

Carlos Ruiz Massieu, the Secretary-General’s Special Representative for Haiti and head of BINUH, said the mission’s work depends on one essential ingredient: trust.

“There is trust in the UN,” he said, adding that there is also trust that special political missions “can deliver.” That confidence, he stressed, is something to recognize, cherish and strengthen.

Rarely in the headlines

By the end of 2025, the United Nations had 40 special political missions operating around the world. Today, they are increasingly focused on targeted political tasks: conflict prevention, mediation, regional diplomacy and support for peace processes tailored to specific contexts.

Their work is often invisible by design. They operate through quiet diplomacy, confidential contacts and patient engagement with governments, conflict parties, regional organizations and civil society.

Shamala Kandiah Thompson, from the independent Security Council Report publication, said these missions have “quietly become an indispensable instrument for conflict prevention and political engagement.” They may be less visible than peacekeeping operations, she said, but they are “no less essential.”

Looking ahead, DiCarlo said the publication is not only a record of the past, but also a reminder of what is possible.

“Even in the most difficult of circumstances, dialogue can open doors, patience can build trust, and diplomacy can change the course of history,” she said.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the significant and often underappreciated role of UN special political missions in preventing conflicts, supporting peace processes, and helping fragile states transition to peace and stability. This represents a broad, meaningful positive impact on global peace and security, with detailed historical context and examples demonstrating the effectiveness and adaptability of diplomacy as a tool for conflict prevention and resolution.
articles.personality_title: How UN’s quiet diplomacy helps prevent conflicts worldwide
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context**
Since its creation in 1948, the United Nations (UN) has worked to keep peace around the world. One important way it does this is through special political missions. These missions use talks and negotiation instead of soldiers or weapons to stop conflicts before they get worse.

**What happened**
The UN recently shared a full review of these special political missions, showing how they have helped prevent wars and support peace in many countries from 1948 to today. The first mission was in Palestine, led by a Swedish diplomat, to help stop fighting. Since then, missions have helped countries like Libya become independent, supported peace talks in Afghanistan and Iraq, and assisted fragile countries like Haiti with political talks and elections.

**Impact**
These missions are different from UN peacekeeping troops because they work quietly and use diplomacy. They help prevent conflict, support peace agreements, and assist with political changes without weapons. Their flexibility lets them do many tasks, like monitoring ceasefires or helping create new governments. This approach has helped many countries avoid violence and build peace even during difficult times like the Cold War.

**What's next step**
By the end of 2025, there will be about 40 of these missions worldwide, focusing on specific political problems. The UN plans to keep using this quiet, patient diplomacy because it builds trust and opens doors to peace. This shows that even when problems seem hard, talking and working together can change the future.

**One-sentence takeaway**
The UN’s special political missions quietly use diplomacy to prevent conflicts and support peace in many countries around the world.
articles.personality_title_fr: Comment la diplomatie discrète de l’ONU aide à prévenir les conflits dans le monde
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte**
Depuis sa création en 1948, l’Organisation des Nations Unies (ONU) œuvre pour maintenir la paix dans le monde. Une de ses méthodes importantes est les missions politiques spéciales. Ces missions utilisent le dialogue et la négociation plutôt que des soldats ou des armes pour empêcher les conflits de s’aggraver.

**Ce qui s’est passé**
L’ONU a récemment publié un rapport complet sur ces missions politiques spéciales, montrant comment elles ont aidé à prévenir des guerres et soutenir la paix dans plusieurs pays de 1948 à aujourd’hui. La première mission a eu lieu en Palestine, menée par un diplomate suédois, pour aider à arrêter les combats. Depuis, ces missions ont aidé des pays comme la Libye à devenir indépendants, soutenu des négociations de paix en Afghanistan et en Irak, et assisté des pays fragiles comme Haïti dans leurs dialogues politiques et leurs élections.

**Impact**
Ces missions sont différentes des opérations de maintien de la paix car elles agissent en toute discrétion et par la diplomatie. Elles aident à prévenir les conflits, soutiennent les accords de paix et accompagnent les changements politiques sans recourir aux armes. Leur flexibilité leur permet d’accomplir diverses tâches, comme surveiller les cessez-le-feu ou aider à créer de nouveaux gouvernements. Cette approche a permis à de nombreux pays d’éviter la violence et de construire la paix, même pendant des périodes difficiles comme la Guerre froide.

**Prochaines étapes**
D’ici la fin de 2025, environ 40 de ces missions seront actives à travers le monde, se concentrant sur des problèmes politiques spécifiques. L’ONU prévoit de continuer à utiliser cette diplomatie discrète et patiente, car elle crée la confiance et ouvre des portes vers la paix. Cela montre que même quand les problèmes semblent compliqués, le dialogue et la coopération peuvent changer l’avenir.

**Une phrase pour retenir**
Les missions politiques spéciales de l’ONU utilisent discrètement la diplomatie pour prévenir les conflits et soutenir la paix dans de nombreux pays.
articles.personality_title_es: Cómo la diplomacia silenciosa de la ONU ayuda a prevenir conflictos en el mundo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto**
Desde su creación en 1948, las Naciones Unidas (ONU) han trabajado para mantener la paz mundial. Una forma importante de hacerlo es mediante misiones políticas especiales. Estas misiones usan el diálogo y la negociación en lugar de soldados o armas para detener los conflictos antes de que empeoren.

**Qué pasó**
La ONU compartió recientemente una revisión completa de estas misiones políticas especiales, mostrando cómo han ayudado a prevenir guerras y apoyar la paz en muchos países desde 1948 hasta hoy. La primera misión fue en Palestina, liderada por un diplomático sueco, para ayudar a detener los combates. Desde entonces, las misiones han ayudado a países como Libia a lograr la independencia, apoyaron negociaciones de paz en Afganistán e Irak, y asistieron a países frágiles como Haití con diálogos políticos y elecciones.

**Impacto**
Estas misiones son diferentes de las fuerzas de paz de la ONU porque trabajan de manera discreta y usan la diplomacia. Ayudan a prevenir conflictos, respaldar acuerdos de paz y asistir en cambios políticos sin armas. Su flexibilidad les permite realizar muchas tareas, como monitorear ceses al fuego o ayudar a crear nuevos gobiernos. Este enfoque ha ayudado a muchos países a evitar la violencia y construir la paz, incluso en tiempos difíciles como la Guerra Fría.

**Próximo paso**
Para fines de 2025, habrá unas 40 de estas misiones en todo el mundo, enfocadas en problemas políticos específicos. La ONU planea seguir usando esta diplomacia silenciosa y paciente porque genera confianza y abre puertas hacia la paz. Esto muestra que, aunque los problemas parezcan difíciles, hablar y trabajar juntos puede cambiar el futuro.

**Una frase para recordar**
Las misiones políticas especiales de la ONU usan la diplomacia para prevenir conflictos y apoyar la paz en muchos países del mundo.
